questions.prompt: What is the recognizable signal that a problem fits the 0/1 Knapsack DP shape?

options.text: A fixed resource budget (capacity, weight limit, target sum) combined with per-item binary include/exclude decisions, each item usable at most once.
Any problem involving an array of integers, regardless of what operation is being performed on it.
Any problem that asks for a maximum or minimum value, regardless of structure.
The presence of two nested loops in the naive brute-force solution.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's specific diagnostic for this shape: a budget being consumed plus binary per-item decisions is what defines the (item index, remaining budget) state — subset-sum and bounded coin-change are named as the same shape precisely because they share this structure, not because they're superficially similar. (b) is far too broad — countless non-knapsack problems involve integer arrays. (c) optimization objective alone doesn't identify a specific DP shape; many different shapes optimize a max/min. (d) nested loops are an implementation artifact of many different algorithm types, not a reliable signal specific to this shape.

questions.prompt: Longest Common Subsequence and Edit Distance are described as 'the same shape with different per-cell logic.' What do they share, and what differs?

options.text: Both use state (position in sequence A, position in sequence B); LCS's per-cell rule has 2 options (match-and-extend, or skip from one side), while edit distance's has 3 (insert, delete, substitute) — the state shape is identical, only the combine rule at each cell differs.
They are unrelated problems that happen to both be solvable with dynamic programming, with no structural connection.
LCS operates on two sequences while edit distance operates on only one.
Edit distance is a special case of LCS that only applies to strings of equal length.

questions.explanation: This is exactly the chapter's point about recognizing shared shape despite surface differences: same two-index state axis structure, different transition rule per cell — recognizing this connection is what lets solving one problem transfer insight to the other, rather than treating them as two separately memorized techniques. (b) understates a deliberate, chapter-highlighted structural connection. (c) is false; edit distance also operates on two sequences (the source and target strings) — that's precisely why it shares the two-index state with LCS. (d) is false on both counts — edit distance is not a 'special case' of LCS, and neither requires equal-length inputs.

questions.prompt: The chapter claims grid DP (e.g. minimum path sum moving right/down) is 'structurally the same idea' as LCS's two-sequence state. What's the connection?

options.text: Grid DP's (row, column) state and combine-from-valid-predecessors logic is the same two-axis 'combine from prior state' structure as LCS's (position in A, position in B) — the grid's row and column axes play the role LCS's two sequences play.
There is no real connection; the chapter's claim is a loose analogy without structural basis.
Grid DP always requires exactly the same number of rows and columns as the two sequences in an LCS problem.
Grid DP and LCS solve identical problems and produce identical answers on the same input.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit unification move: both patterns share the two-axis-state, combine-from-predecessors shape (dp[r][c] depends on dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1] in grid DP; dp[i][j] depends on dp[i-1][j-1]/dp[i-1][j]/dp[i][j-1] in LCS) — recognizing this turns two memorized categories into one applied pattern. (b) dismisses a genuine structural parallel the chapter draws deliberately, not a vague comparison. (c) there's no such dimensional-matching requirement; grid dimensions and sequence lengths are independent, unrelated quantities. (d) they solve different PROBLEMS (path-cost vs. subsequence-length) despite sharing computational structure — the connection is about SHAPE, not about producing the same answers.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter's diagnostic workflow for a novel DP problem, what should you identify FIRST — before trying to match it to a memorized problem type?

options.text: What decision repeats (one state axis) and what running constraint needs tracking (the other axis, or an additional one) — reading the problem for its state, rather than pattern-matching to a remembered problem.
Whether the problem can be solved with a greedy algorithm instead, since greedy is always preferable when applicable.
The exact Big-O time complexity the final solution must achieve.
Whether the problem has appeared in a well-known algorithms textbook before.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's stated diagnostic sequence and its explicit goal: building the skill of reading state axes directly from a problem's structure is what generalizes to UNSEEN problems, versus pattern-matching to memorized ones, which fails the moment a genuinely novel problem appears. (b) greedy-vs-DP is a different chapter's question (chapter 7) and isn't the first diagnostic step for a problem already suspected to need DP. (c) target complexity is a downstream concern once a correct approach is found, not the starting diagnostic. (d) relying on textbook recognition is precisely the memorization-dependent approach the chapter is trying to move you away from.

questions.prompt: A candidate identifies a DP state with too FEW dimensions for a new problem. What is the resulting failure mode, as described in the chapter?

options.text: An incorrect recurrence — information that was discarded (not tracked in the state) turns out to matter for correctness, producing wrong answers even though the code runs and the table fills without error.
The table becomes infeasibly large to compute or store.
The algorithm becomes slower but still produces correct results.
A compilation error, since insufficient state dimensions cannot be expressed in code.

questions.explanation: This is the specific failure the chapter distinguishes from its opposite: too little state means the recurrence silently loses information it needed, producing a plausible-looking but WRONG answer — a correctness bug, not a performance or feasibility one. (b) is the OPPOSITE failure mode (too MUCH state), explicitly named as the other side of this same trade-off in the chapter. (c) incorrectly assumes the algorithm still produces correct results — the chapter is explicit that insufficient state breaks correctness, not just speed. (d) is false; under-dimensioned state compiles and runs fine — the bug is purely semantic/logical, which is what makes it dangerous (it doesn't announce itself).

questions.prompt: A DP problem's naturally-identified state has so many dimensions (or such a large range) that the resulting table would be infeasibly large to build. What does the chapter suggest as the direction to look for a fix?

options.text: Look for a smarter, more compact state representation that captures the same necessary information more compactly — the chapter points specifically to bitmask-based state compression (covered in a later chapter) as one concrete technique for this.
Abandon dynamic programming entirely and switch to a greedy approach, which always has smaller state requirements.
Increase the table size further to ensure correctness, accepting the larger memory cost as unavoidable.
This situation cannot be resolved; DP is fundamentally inapplicable whenever the naive state space is large.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit forward-reference and guidance: state-space explosion is a recognized, addressable problem, and compressing the state representation (rather than abandoning the approach) is the standard fix — bitmasking is named as one concrete example technique covered later in this course. (b) greedy algorithms solve a fundamentally different (and narrower) class of problems than DP; switching approaches isn't a free substitution, and greedy doesn't 'always' have smaller state requirements since it doesn't use tabulated state the same way at all. (c) is backwards — the goal is REDUCING state size, not accepting or growing an infeasible one. (d) overstates the limitation; the chapter explicitly frames this as a solvable problem via better state representation, not a hard wall for DP.
